comment_content: The content is mainly the basic python programming knowledge, if you have learnt coding and programming before, it would be easy to handle, but i think with assistance of AI, there should be not difficulty on understanding the code. AI can handle all the content in this course. Topics are variable, type, for loop, while loop, recursion, class, oop.
comment_teaching: The teaching is fair, if you like to attend the lecture, go for it, COMP department professors are quite good at teaching, but if you have learnt all the concept of basic python programming, it would be a little bit necessary, since everything is easy, you can just search online or ask AI, i think that would be more efficient for learning. Remember practice is the key to success. Don't just read the lecture note and think you know everything, it doesn't work. At least type something by yourself.
comment_workload: The workload is fair, about 1 PA a week, the PA is easy to finish, just about the topic that you learn that week, nothing special, just remember to do it and everything would get full marks on that usually.
comment_grading: The grading is fair, since most of the student taking this course is from school of engineering, if you have no coding experience, it would be one the of biggest disadvantage by comparing to other student. But i think the key is to be careful enough, the content is easy but the exam content can be tricky. Understanding what the question requires you to implement is important, you can treat the exam as english reading exam, lots of words. Be aware of your time management. You may not able to finish the exam if you struggle in one particular questions, especially recursion.

comment_workload: There are a few assignments that are mandatory to participate in, while the rest are optional. The content is quite simple, and the instructions are very clear. Some assignments can even be perfectly completed by simply following the instructions rigorously. Additionally, online guidance is provided, making the difficulty very low.It should be noted that even if not mandatory, the homework that needs to be submitted will also be one of the topics for the exam. Therefore, even if you don't complete the homework, you still need to review it thoroughly.
comment_grading: There are two types of point systems. The first type is where both the midterm and final exams contribute to the final grade. The second type only requires the final exam score to be part of the final grade. The remaining portion is from assignments. I didn't take the midterm exam because it conflicted with other exams I had. There is no make-up exam mechanism for the midterm exam in this course. So, if you really want to fully participate in the entire course, you can discuss the situation with other course instructors to see if it's possible to reschedule other exams.

comment_content:

comment_teaching:

He may go a bit fast at times but his content averages 20 slides per 50 minute lecture so it is not much to catch up on or ask about after class. He is also very open to answering questions and seems to have a real passion to teach
comment_workload: Assignments may take a few hours to complete for those who lack core knowledge already but assignments are not given weekly and is very useful as a form of studying the understanding of the code.
comment_grading:
Grading is heavy on the final but as long as you do well in your assignments(90%>) it isn't too hard to get a B as long as you study a decent amount for the exams themselves.

Calculation is also varies in your favour depending on whether you do better in your final or midterm(automatically)

comment_content: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to Python programming, starting from basic syntax and control structures (if–else statements, loops, functions) and gradually moving on to data structures such as lists, tuples, and dictionaries. The use of Turtle and PyGame libraries helps students understand graphical programming, event handling, and basic UI concepts, which makes the learning process more engaging. More advanced topics, including recursion, file input/output, and basic object-oriented programming, are briefly introduced toward the end. While not all Python features are covered, the scope is appropriate for an introductory-level course.
comment_teaching: Lectures are conducted entirely in English, using relatively simple language, although the speaking pace can be fast at times. The quality of teaching materials is high, especially the lecture PowerPoint slides, which are clear, well-organized, and closely aligned with the examinable content. The course website is well maintained and updated weekly. Lab instructors explain concepts step by step and often demonstrate code directly, which is particularly helpful for students with little or no programming background.
comment_workload: The overall workload is moderate and manageable. Most learning comes from reviewing lecture slides and understanding the provided code examples rather than extensive memorization. Labs are held weekly and are mostly ungraded, but they are useful for practicing core concepts and preparing for assignments. As long as students keep up with the weekly materials and do not leave assignments until the last minute, the time commitment required for this course is reasonable.
comment_grading: Grading is based on a mid-term exam, a final exam, and two assignments. Exams are open-book but still require a solid understanding of program structure, syntax, and data structures, especially list operations. Assignments are generally graded fairly and generously if the required functionality is correctly implemented. Overall, the grading scheme is reasonable, and students who consistently follow the course materials and practice coding should be able to achieve good results.

AI is helpful for debugging, but I don't think you can completely rely on. Better copy all the code given in the lab video before using AI, I would save a lot of time for you for drrafting prompt.

- Final (65% / 45%)

For the final, I got a few marks above the median since it was apparently harder than the midterm.

If you're aiming high, I guess it would be difficult cuz many students have python background and got almost full marks in the exams.The SD is quite high for all exams. But it is easy to pass and guarantee a B range if you put in adequate effort.

comment_teaching:
I'd say James is a good professor. Quite humorous(dry humour), I enjoyed his lessons. I feel comfortable to do my own thing even though the class regularly has <20 people. Feel free to ask him any questions after class.

I sit one of Gibson's lessons, he is also a good prof and I can tell why his classes are popular. However, I personally prefer James' teaching style more. Gibson's voice felt more aggressive(?), and he talks quite fast, so a lot of info is entering my brain, which I struggle slightly to catch up. James' pace is more suitable for me.
comment_workload: There are two assignments that count for the course grade. The workload is manageable, with one lab per week, though not every lab needs to be submitted. Sometimes, the labs are related to the assignment. I suggest finishing the work given every week, whether or not it needs to be submitted. Instead of doing it in the last few days, because it is MUCH longer than you think... (This is how I got 20/100 for Assignment 2, please learn from my mistake)
comment_grading:
The midterm is quite simple, but I suspect they want everyone to fail their finals because WHAT WAS THAT???

Midterm: 78/100, average: 55.9, sd: 24.7
Finals: 47/100, average: 43.7, sd: 20.5
I got a B for this course, but I think I should've gotten at least B+ if I hadn't butchered my Assignment 2 with a 20/100.....

On the other hand, getting 100/100 for all your labs and assignments is very easy. As long as you follow the instructions and check the marking scheme carefully, full mark should be expected.

The course covers not only basic syntax but also introduces useful libraries like math and random, preparing students for more advanced topics.

comment_grading:
Two grading methods.
Method 1: midterm 24%, lab projects 36%, final exam 40%
Method 2: midterm 0%, lab projects 36%, final exam 40%

It’s easy to get a good grade. You can refer to your notes or textbooks during the midterm and final exams, but you still need to understand the concepts of programming first. Also, pay attention to the time. If you don’t know the answer of a question, skip it and come back to it later.

comment_content:
introductory programming course using Python 3 and PyGame as the foundation, covering both programming basics and simple UI concepts.
Programming basics, Python functions:
• Conditionals (if/elif/else), loops (while, for…in)
• Turtle library basics and drawing with loops
• PyGame MIDI and time control
• Data structures: list, tuple, dictionary
• List and string slicing, common methods (append, insert, remove, etc.)
• Functions, scope, recursion, stack
• File read/write, type()
• Dictionary operations
comment_teaching:

comment_workload:
• Reading lecture slides and trying code examples is sufficient for ~90% in the mid-term and ~80% in the final.
• Preparing slides before class (hard/soft copy) is helpful, especially for beginners.
• Review after lectures should focus on syntax and structure.
comment_grading:
1. Mid-term Exam (20%) – open book, 1 hr, Week 7 (~40–45% of course).
• If skipped or if counting it lowers your grade, weight becomes 0%.
2. Final Exam (40%) – open book, 2.5 hr, covers the whole course.
• If no mid-term taken, or mid-term lowers your grade, weight becomes 60%.
3. Assignment 1: Music System (20%) – command-line program generating MIDI from text files.
• Lab 3 & 4 completion = 30% of A1; extra features = 70%.
4. Assignment 2: Game Project (20%) – Turtle-based “Space Invaders” game with event handling.
• Lab 7 & 8 completion = 34% of A2; extra features = 66%.
5. Assignment Bonus Point (2%) – awarded only if A1 + A2 < 40%. Earned by answering in-class questions (ping pong ball system).

comment_content: This is a introductory computer course about Python programming. As it is for beginner, its content is not deep and complex.  However, if you are not good at or interested in programming, you will find it's complicated or boring. For me, it arouses my interest in game programming, as there is a assignment about creating a treasure game.
comment_teaching: Every lesson has 50 mins only and it will be a new chapter, so the pitch is a little bit fast, especially to those who have not ever learn programming before. The teaching way is like a talk, explaining the ppt and sometimes demonstrates through the Python. However, it is somewhat difficult to understand. Besides lecture, there are tutorial video and zoom class. These tutorial classes are for homework guidance so they are recommended. Also, watch recordings after each lecture and do exercises released from each lecture. Practice will help for your understanding.
comment_workload:
There are 5 programming assignments. Remember to hand in those assignments on time.  One midterm and one final exam. Also, there are two lecture classes each week, and each lesson is 50 mins only. There are also tutorial class. However, all the lecture and tutorial classes didn't take attendance and there are recordings. Therefore, the workload is super low.
But to get a better performance in midterm and final exam, do past exam paper as much as you can. The format is similar within  years. You will find those past paper from CANVAS, the instructor will release for you.
comment_grading: The Homework assignments account for a great percentage to the overall grading. Most of students will get full mark so the overall grading will not be bad. But remember to hand in the HW on time, as late for 1 second will be graded as 0.  Other percentage goes to Midterm and Final Exam. There are two grading scheme : count both midterm and final exam in or only count final exam . Therefore, if you perform not well in midterm, you will another chance in final. Also, the median for midterm and final is 50% to 60% only. If you meets median and get full mark in assignments, you will get at least B or B+.

comment_content:
Fundamental Python Concepts:
Covers essentials such as if/else statements, for loops, lists, dictionaries, basic classes, methods/functions, and recursion.

Turtle Graphics:
Introduces the Turtle graphics library, which allows you to create drawings by controlling a "turtle" (think of it as a pen or brush) using code.

Difficulty:
The material should be very straightforward if you have any prior programming experience. However, take care with data types—Python is dynamically typed (and you won’t be using or tested on type hints in exams), so watch out for type-related pitfalls.
comment_teaching: The teacher has a great sense of humor, and the class is generally quite enjoyable. However, it can sometimes take extra effort to fully grasp what he's teaching.
comment_workload:
Personally, I completed all the self-exercises and practiced with all the past papers for both the midterm and final exams. It can be time-consuming, but it’s extremely helpful—I highly recommend doing all of them, as the exam questions are often quite similar.

The midterm is relatively easy, so aim to get a perfect score. However, the final exam is much more challenging; make sure you prepare thoroughly and pay close attention!
comment_grading:
Overall, I’m quite pleased with my performance. Although I lost around 10% on the assignments, I still managed to finish strong.

Final Exam Statistics:

Average: ~62.3%
Standard Deviation: ~17.8%
Highest Score: 98.5%
Lowest Score: ~12.4%
There are two grading systems: one includes the midterm, and one doesn’t. Using the first scheme, my scores break down as follows:

Assignments (36% of total): 88.5%
Midterm (24% of total): 95%
Final Exam (40% of total): 82%
Final Score: 87.1%
Letter Grade: A

comment_content:
The course offers a solid introduction to Python, with a good balance of practical tasks. Largely beginner-friendly, making it easy to grasp, even with no prior programming experience.

Covers essential topics like: loops, functions, recursion, and data types, with some going into basic object-oriented programming and handling files
comment_teaching: Lectures are well-organized, and supplementary materials are always available for self-learning so I liked it. Gibson Lam’s style is engaging and approachable, though there may be moments where he gets a bit carried away with jokes.
comment_workload:
Generally manageable, with most courses consisting of weekly lab assignments that help reinforce concepts learned in class. The labs are typically not overwhelming and can usually be completed within a day, though the difficulty increases as it progresses.

The flexibility in deadlines and lab submission dates allows everyone to manage their time well, but staying on top of assignments is key.
comment_grading:
Grading is competitive but fair. Many courses have flexible grading schemes, which let you choose whether to include low midterm scores in their final grade calculations. Lab assignments play a significant role in determining grades, often making up a substantial portion of the final grade, though their weight varies.

I think it’s possible to get an A with consistent effort. Must be diligent with practice to do well, as exams caught me and my friends off guard.

comment_grading:
Grade = (Graded labs + Midterm + Final) OR (Graded labs + Final), whichever is higher.
Pretty polarising. The mean for both midterm and finals is around the ~55/100 while the SD is ~20.
You get an A if you get around 1 SD above mean which is the standard imo.
You can choose to not count your midterm score if you do badly in midterms, so you can afford to do badly in the midterms for whatever reason.

comment_workload: The workload is negligible. The class attendance is not mandatory and you can view any of the prof's class recordings if you want to. Also for our final exam, they brought many lab-related context questions (even for the labs that were not hand-in) so make sure you do your labs and review them before the exam.
comment_grading:
Assessments:
There are two ways to assess each student:
• Scheme A (more midterm %): Midterm 24%, Lab projects 36%, Final exam 40%
• Scheme B (less midterm %): Midterm 0%, Lab projects 42%, Final exam 58%
The course will automatically choose the highest mark of these two assessment schemes.

Only 3 Lab Assignments are hand-in (out of 6 or 7 Lab Assignments in total) and only those will be counted in the final grading.

I think the two grading schemes are actually pretty helpful, some people got mean in midterms but got 1 S.D+ in finals and got an A in the end. So this grading method really helps in giving students a second chance.

comment_content:
Background: DSE ICT lv5 with Elective D (programming elective) (before major syllabus change)

It is called turtle course for a reason. By the end of course, you will be so traumatized by turtle that the only thing you can remember is import turtle. I get that including turtle may seem to be more visual appealing instead of constantly staring at the boring IDLE but turtle terrorizes the questions to some stupid ass geometry problems. Some actually more relevant content (by relevant i mean in CS context) , like Object Oriented Programming and Recursion, is intended to be taught in just 1 lesson. Moreover, basic algorithms are not included in this course. These factors makes the effectiveness in introducing CS to students questionable.

TLDR: An okay introduction to python but a BAD introduction to CS

p.s. I heard there would be COMP1023 offered in 2526 fall onwards which is much better than this turtle course.
comment_teaching: Gibson is definitely a good guy. He has his own style of teaching and prepares his own materials. He is able to explain concepts clearly and maintain an engaging tone throughout the lecture, making the lectures enjoyable to attend.
comment_workload: Light workload. There are 6 labs in total but only 3 of them are hand in labs. The content of the labs may be tested in midterms and finals. Plus the lab tasks arent that difficult and time consuming to complete so there is no reason not to finish all the labs.
comment_grading:
There are 2 grading schemes:
1. Lab work 36%, Midterm 24%, Finals 40%
2. Lab work 42%, Midterm 0%, Finals 58%

My grade:
Midterm: 51/100
3 labs: 100/100
            100/100
            100/100
Final: 74/100
Letter grade: A+

You should really be getting full marks in labs because you can literately consult everything, check answer with friends before handing in the labs.

This semester (or maybe onwards) all tests are f2f. Gibson said last semester (2425 fall) can still justify online tests due to having 1000+ ppl enrolled in the course but this semester there is only 500+ students enrolled in this course, which cannot justify an online exam. Plus the school wants to avoid having online exams if possible.

You lose the ability to use IDLE in f2f exams but you can still bring your own notes (format, content is not limited). Although the use of notes are limited to checking for syntax error and reminding yourself how to use the functions, I would still recommend you to type your own notes if you have the time since it is a good opportunity to review the lecture ppt and make sure that you know how to use the functions, codes, etc. Also, you can find the relevant information in the notes faster and easier with your own notes, which is quite important especially when time is limited

Midterm is HELL. I wouldnt blame on the f2f exams since written programming exams arent a novel thing to me with my ICT experience but you only have a little time to rush through that many questions. I bet if this midterm is done online, it would still be hell. The mean is so bad (26.53) that gibson decided to dedicate 1 lesson to explain the midterm paper (gibson expected the mean to be 50 💀).

Final is much better than midterm. Maybe there is more questions (i forgor already) but i feel like more time is given per question. I can actually finish with 15-20 minutes to spare.

There is around 1 assignment per week, but only like half of them are compulsory. The remaining ones are optional and are not required to be submitted.

The lab assignments are EASY. A little too easy. They have tutorial videos to teach you step by step, even for very easy parts. The instructions are quite detailed.

Only one thing: for some very extreme cases or special cases, it happens that their instructions might be a little unclear. However, I think their testcases won't test that much.
comment_grading:
In this semester, the exams are computer-based. You can make use of Python during the exam to compute the answers. The exam is not very simple and not very hard (the time limit is tight though). But somehow, there will be a lot of people getting 100 marks. And there is always a group of people getting 0 marks. The standard deviation is quite large.

Unlike COMP2011 (where the total score is divided into many small parts), in COMP1021, each answer accounts for 6%-9% of the whole paper. You type your answers into the computer during the exam. So, the computer can catch your mistake even if you misspell one letter. (Yes, I lost 8 marks because of one letter)

For both the midterm and the final, I did not get full marks (maybe around 8X or 9X marks). I still got an A+ finally.

comment_workload:
The possible ways you can guarantee an acceptable score is to legit do the labs with your own hands and understanding it, as these will appear in your midterms and finals.

Second of all, start by doing past papers that are written, rather than virtual on computers. I'm not saying not to do them, but do them after you have done the written past papers. Written papers actually give you an approximate image on what the actual exam is going to look like.

Lastly, prepare your printed materials early. DO NOT THINK YOU CAN CARRY THE BOOK AND ACE THE EXAM. It is called open book, open notes for a reason. You won't expect to find your answers quickly inside the book. Prepare things like a summary on how each code works in practice, and print out the lab codes, if you feel like so.
comment_grading:
Two grading schemes, which for this course, I don't find it much attractive.

1. Lab work 36%, Midterm 24%, Finals 40%
2. Lab work 42%, Midterm 0%, Finals 58%

The reason is purely because after this semester onwards, exams are taken in face-to-face written mode, rather than the 24 Fall Semester, exams are taken on a computer virtually. It's an open book open notes exam, but the fact that we had to use human brains to understand the few lines of code in such limited time is basically a pain to the ass.

3 lab works will be graded out of the 5-7 labs released throughout the semester. For us, it was Lab 1, 4 and 5 (Guessing game, Lava game, Music system), each worth 12% or 14% of your final score.

Midterms and finals, as mentioned, are written after so long of virtual exams. Several reasons lead to such a low mean and distribution being that right skewed, mostly being time constraints and the diverse background of students taking this course.

Score distribution (LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High):
Lab 1: 97.5/89.3/100/100/100
Lab 4: 90/87.46/100/100/100
Lab 5: 60/72.9/90/100/100
Midterms: 13/26.53/23/34/94
Finals: 28/41.47/39/52/98

62% was my final score and got a B. Fair grading? Not my say, but at the very least, do well with your finals.

comment_content: The course covered some basic concepts in computer science, but I felt it was a bit too focused on turtle graphics. While it was a fun way to learn, it limited the depth of other topics. We touched on important concepts like if statements, for loops, lists, dictionaries, functions, and recursion, but I think there could have been more variety. It would have been great to explore additional programming paradigms or real-world applications of Python to give us a broader understanding of the language and its capabilities. Overall, a more balanced approach would enhance the learning experience.
comment_teaching: You can definitely manage in this course without attending lectures. I didn’t go to any of them and just worked through the PowerPoint presentations and completed the mandatory labs. The materials are pretty well organized, so if you ever feel like you need clarification, you can always watch the lectures later. As long as you stay on top of the labs and engage with the content, you’ll be fine. It’s a flexible approach that allows you to learn at your own pace.
comment_workload: The course had a pretty light workload, with only 3 mandatory labs out of a total of 6. I’d definitely recommend doing the optional labs as well, since the final exam referenced some of them. If you skip those labs, you might find yourself struggling to answer some of the final questions. Overall, the labs are manageable and a great way to reinforce what you've learned, so taking the time to complete all of them will really pay off in the end.
comment_grading: The grading scale in this course feels a bit off. The gap between the B and C ranges is quite wide, making it challenging to get a solid grade. Achieving an A is tough since many students already have a good grasp of the concepts before starting. The mean score for the midterm was around 50-55, and the final was similar, with a mean of about 50. On the bright side, the labs are pretty straightforward, you can usually tackle most of them in just a few hours, and some can even be finished in an hour. Overall, while the labs are manageable, the grading can be pretty competitive, especially for those aiming for top marks.

comment_content: The constant is a basic introduction to python. Nothing very hard to understand. I was a complete beginner, but just weekly practice and not falling behind on lectures I was able to understand all the concepts. The turtle aspect of the syllabus can be quite tedious but the rest is fine.
comment_teaching: LAM was an amazing teacher. Although he speaks kind of fast, he is easy to understand. Uses good analogies to explain and very friendly. It’s very easy to ask him questions after class
comment_workload: Very acceptable to be honest. The labs are fine except if u leave them last minute. 1 midterm and 1 final should be manageable.
comment_grading: The grading of the course itself is fine. I ended up with a B+. But the curve is really hard. As many people with prior knowledge to python take this course. The average is skewed making it hard for beginners to do well. Everyone tries to scare you with the grading of this course, but just don’t make dumb mistakes and you should be fine. Try to get 100 on the labs because they are very easy. And then do your best on midterm and final. I was above mean by half SD on the midterm and like 5 points above mean on final. Plus my labs were all max and ended up with B+

comment_content: The content is just ok. It is not hard to understand the lecture content. Lessons can be skipped as you can read lecture notes yourselves. The concept are not too difficult. comment_content:
Areas taught before the midterm were quite easy and beginner-friendly, but those after the midterm are difficult (e.g. recursion, file handling)

Some optional interactive tasks were given along with the lecture videos to consolidate your learning.

If you have a clear logic, you will find the course easy. As I don't have any common sense nor logic, I found the content challenging yet interesting.
